name: Hollow Earth Theory
description: The Hollow Earth Theory proposes that the Earth is entirely hollow or contains a substantial interior space. Various versions of the theory exist, but they generally involve an inner sun, habitable inner continents, and entry points at the North and South Poles. The theory has roots in ancient mythology and philosophical speculation, gaining modern traction in the 17th century and becoming popular in the 19th and 20th centuries through the writings of authors like John Cleves Symmes Jr. and Edgar Allan Poe (though Poe's work was fictional). Proponents often cite anecdotal evidence, supposed inconsistencies in geological data, and perceived anomalies in polar explorations as support. Mainstream science overwhelmingly rejects the Hollow Earth Theory. Geological and geophysical data, including seismic wave analysis, gravity measurements, and studies of the Earth's magnetic field, consistently demonstrate that the Earth is composed of distinct layers: a solid crust, a mantle (mostly solid), a liquid outer core, and a solid inner core. There is no scientific evidence to support the existence of large voids or habitable spaces within the Earth. The theory is generally considered pseudoscience and lacks empirical validation.
subtitle: Earth's interior: A hollow world?

claims.claim: The Earth is hollow with openings at the poles.
claims.evidence: Admiral Byrd's alleged diary entries about entering the inner Earth; unexplained magnetic anomalies at the poles.
claims.criticism: Byrd's diary entries are contested; magnetic anomalies are explained by known geophysical processes related to the Earth's magnetic field.

claims.claim: An inner sun illuminates the inner Earth.
claims.evidence: Accounts of warm air and unusual cloud formations emanating from the polar regions.
claims.criticism: These phenomena are explained by normal atmospheric conditions and geothermal activity; no evidence of an inner sun exists.

claims.claim: Advanced civilizations inhabit the inner Earth.
claims.evidence: Legends and folklore about subterranean realms and beings; supposed contactee stories.
claims.criticism: These accounts are unsubstantiated and lack credible evidence; they are often based on speculation and fantasy.

keyFigures.name: John Cleves Symmes Jr.
keyFigures.role: Early proponent of the theory, popularized it in the 19th century

keyFigures.name: Leonard Euler
keyFigures.role: Early scientific speculation about a hollow earth

keyFigures.name: Edmund Halley
keyFigures.role: Proposed a hollow earth model with multiple concentric spheres to explain compass variations

keyFigures.name: Charles Hapgood
keyFigures.role: Authored 'Maps of the Ancient Sea Kings,' sometimes cited as evidence, though his focus was on pole shifts

keyFigures.name: David Stevenson
keyFigures.role: Geophysicist; provides mainstream scientific explanations of earth's structure

mythologyConnections: Various indigenous beliefs about earth spirits and subterranean worlds

timeline.event: Edmund Halley proposes a hollow Earth model to explain compass variations.
John Cleves Symmes Jr. circulates his 'Hollow Earth Circular,' advocating for polar expeditions.
Jules Verne publishes 'Journey to the Center of the Earth,' popularizing the idea in fiction.
Richard E. Byrd's alleged diary entries about entering the inner Earth surface after flying into a polar opening. These entries are disputed.
The theory persists in various forms within conspiracy theories and alternative beliefs.

sources.author: Verne, Jules
sources.work: Journey to the Center of the Earth
sources.title: Verne, Jules

sources.work: Fads and Fallacies in the Name of Science
